content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
July 17, 1989 
Honorable Maxine waters 
Assemblywoman, 48th District 
state capitol 
P. o. Box 942849 
Sacramento, CA 
94249-0001 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-89-364 
Dear Assemblywoman Waters: 
You have requested advice with respect to interpretation of 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act,,).l 
Specifically, you have requested advice concerning the limitations 
on and the reporting of payments received in connection with a 
speaking engagement. 
QUESTIONS 
1. 
Would the a payment received in connection with a fellow-
ship award be considered an "honorarium" under the Act? 
2. 
If the payment is considered an honorarium, would the 
amount of $2,500 be in excess of the amount an elected officer can 
receive in a calendar year for honoraria from a single entity? 
3. 
If the payment is considered a fee for services rather 
than a gift or honorarium, what limitations apply? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. 
A payment received in connection with a fellowship award 
would be considered an "honorarium" under the Act if it is 
received for making a speech, participating in a panel or seminar, 
or writing an article or other published work. 
1 
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory references 
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission 
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 
18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 


Our File No. A-89-364 
Page 2 
2. 
An honorarium for a speech, article or published work on 
the governmental process received by an elected officer is limited 
to $1,000 from a single source in a calendar year, excluding 
actual travel expenses and reasonable sUbsistence directly in con-
nection therewith. 
3. 
If the payment is a fee for services rather than a gift 
or honorarium, the $1,000 limit does not apply. 
However, an 
elected officer who claims the payment as "income", instead of a 
gift or honorarium, has the burden of proving that he or she 
provided consideration of equal or greater value than the payment 
received. 
FACTS 
You have been invited by the University of California at 
Santa Cruz to receive the Cowell College Minority Fellowship, 
which is described as an academic fellowship. 
According to the 
information provided, you have been requested to provide one full 
day of speaking engagements at the university. 
The proposed 
schedule sets forth the following activities: 
1) 
A dialogue and meeting with student leaders of color ac-
tive in student organizations for educational rights and multi-
cultural diversity. 
2) 
Public address to the UCSC Student Community and faculty 
at large at Cowell College and award ceremony of the Cowell Fel-
lowship. 
The fellowship award includes a payment to you in the amount 
of $2,500. 
The letter of invitation which you enclosed 
categorizes this payment as an "honorarium." 
ANALYSIS 
Section 85400 provides as follows: 
No elected officeholder shall accept any 
gift or honorarium for any speech, article, or 
published work on a subject relating to the 
governmental process from any single source 
which is in excess of one thousand dollars 
($1,000), in any calendar year, except 
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and 
reasonable sUbsistence in connection 
therewith. 
section 85400. 
As used in Section 85400, the term "honorarium" means any 
payment received for making a speech, participating in a panel or 


OUr File No. A-89-364 
Page 3 
seminar, or writing an article or other published work. 
(Regula-
tion l8540(b) , copyenclosed.)2 
"Speech" refers to a public ad-
dress, oration, or other form of sUbstantive oral presentation. 
(Regulation l8540(d).) 
"Governmental process" is defined as 
including activities related to the official duties of the elected 
officer, or the functions, programs and policies of government. 
(Regulation 18540(e).) 
According to the letter of invitation that you provided, it 
appears that the $2,500 payment that you would receive from the 
university would be for making a public address and/or other 
sUbstantive oral presentations. 
If your speech or presentation 
relates to your official duties or to the functions, programs and 
pOlicies of government, the payment would be subject to the 
limitations set forth in section 85400. 
section 85400 limits payments for speeches on the 
governmental process, whether denominated as gifts or honoraria, 
to $1,000 in any calendar year from any single source, excluding 
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and reasonable 
sUbsistence in connection therewith. 
Therefore, the maximum 
honorarium that you could receive would be $1,000, excluding 
reimbursement for travel and reasonable subsistence. 
"Reasonable 
subsistence" means reasonable payments for necessary meals and 
lodging provided directly in connection with the speech. 
(Foreman 
Advice Letter, No. 1-89-323, copy enclosed.) 
The fact that you would be making a series of oral presenta-
tions does not alter this result. 
An elected officer may not ac-
cept more than a total of $1,000 for any speech or speeches given 
at a single assemblage. 
A single assemblage includes all events 
scheduled for a single conference, convention or similar gather-
ing. 
(Regulation 18540(g).) 
If the fellowship payment is not really an honorarium, but, 
rather, a fee for services, the $1,000 limitation of section 85400 
would not apply. 
Generally a payment for speaking on the 
governmental process is presumed to be a gift. 
If you claim that 
the payment is income, you will have the burden of proving that 
the consideration you provided was of equal or greater value than 
the payment received. 
(Regulation 18540(c).) 
Notwithstanding the above presumption, subsection (g) (2) of 
Regulation 18540 provides that payments received from a bona fide 
educational institution pursuant to a contract for teaching are 
considered "income." 
Public universities such as the University 
of California are specifically included as bona fide institutions. 
(Regulation 18540(g) (2).) 
We do not have sufficient information 
about the fellowship program to determine whether it would qualify 
Regulation 18540 was adopted by the Commission on June 6, 1989, 
and is currently pending review by the Office of Administrative 
Law. 


Our File No. A-89-364 
Page 4 
as a teaching contract. 
If it is a contract for teaching, the 
payment can be considered "income" and the $1,000 limitation of 
Section 85400 will not apply. 
If you have any questions regarding the above, please contact 
me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED/MWE/aa 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
By: 
Margaret W. Ellison 
Counsel, Legal Division 


Ms. Lilly spitz 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J street, suite 800 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Dear Lilly: 
June 12, 1989 
I have enclosed a copy of an invitation from UC Santa Cruz 
asking me to accept their invitation to become a Cowell College 
Minority Fellowship with an "honorarium" of $2,500. 
You can see 
from the enclosed letter the tasks they are asking me to fulfill 
as a recipient of this award. 
My question is whether or not this fellowship would be 
considered an honorarium under Proposition 73 and any regulations 
the Commission has adopted to interpret that section of 
Proposition 73? 
If this fellowship is considered an honorarium, is the 
amount of $2,500 then in excess of the limit one can receive in a 
calendar year from a single entity? 
If this fellowship is not an honorarium, but a fee for 
services, what limitations then apply? 
Thank you in advance for your early response to this letter. 
MW/RC/afc 
48th District 
enclosure 


r1AY 09 '89 14: 40 DIST 48 
1-'.1 
UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SANTA CRUZ 
lIUU:ELEY • :DAVIS • IRVINE • LOS A~GEL1>5 • lUVEl'.Gl!JE • SAN l:)IEGO • SAN FRANCISCO 
SAXTA :SAMARA' &AXTA CR1;;Z; 
COWELL COl-LEOS: 
Assemblywoman Maxine Waters 
7900 South Central Ave. 
LGS Angeles, California 
Dear Assemblywoman Maxine Waters: 
SASH, CRUZ, C~LIFORSIA 95064 
flAY 8 1989 
We are pleased to invite you to receive a University of California 
Academic Fellowship: the Cowell College Minority Fellowship, with an 
honorarium of $2,500 awarded by Cowell's Provosts John Lynch and Mary 
Kay-Gamel. 
' 
Past Fellows inolude: artist Robert Blackburn, aotor/musician Daniel 
Valdez, writer MiChelle Cliff, legislator Miohael Woo, and actor Bruce A. 
Young to the campus in previous years. Eaoh Fellow found the experience 
personally rewarding in oontributing substantially to the academio life of 
our campus community. The colleges and the local community have 
reoelved the Fellows most warmly and with oonsiderable enthusiasm. 
The format of the visit is quite flexible. Fellows normally stay on campus 
as guests of the oollege for two to five weekdays. During this period, they 
meet informally with students and faculty in olasses. They usually 
present a lecture In the college and, if they wish, in the looal community. 
Most of all, Fellows visit and talk with students and faculty on an 
Informal basis in and around the college, 
However recognizing your busy schedule as a legislator and state chair of 
the California Rainbow Coalition, we would hope you could provide at ieast 
one full day of speaking engagements at the university. Here is a proposed 
schedule: 
1) A dialogue and meeting with student leaders 
of color active In student organizations for educational 
rights and multi-cultural diversity. 
2) Public address to the UCSC Student Community and 
faculty at large at Cowell College and award ceremony of 
the Cowell Fellowship to be presented by Provosts John 
Lynch and Mary Kay-Gamel, and African/Black Student 
Assooiation. 


~~y 09 '89 14:41 DIST 48 
p -, 
(IIePltJ£j ~ ilIJi 
We would suggest a visit on the week of May 22 or 2~ 
trying to 
avoid vacations and encroaching on final exam weeks. We very much hope 
you will take the time off In your busy schedule to accept this fellowship 
-2-
and engage in rewarding discussion with our community. 
Sincerely, 
BLt.:; 
Mary Kay Gamel 
Provosts of Cowell College 
Arrangements should be made through the Student Activities Office of 
Cowell College, UCSC; please contaot Steven Morozuml and Angie 
Christman, Student Activities Officers at 408 429-2255 or evenings 408 
423-9646. 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
June 19, 1989 
Honorable Maxine Waters 
Assemblywoman, 48th Distict 
state Capitol 
P.O. Box 942849 
Sacramento, CA 
94249-0001 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-364 
Dear Assemblywoman Waters: 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
was received on June 16, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact Margaret Ellison an attorney in the Legal 
Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you-should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
KED:plh 
Very truly yours, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • 
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • (916)322,5660 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Bette Racki 
Deputy City Clerk 
23 Russell Blvd. 
Davis, CA 
95616 
June 21, 1989 
Re: 
Combined campaign Report 
Our File No. A-89-365 
Dear Ms. Racki: 
This is to confirm advice provided to you on June 19, 1989, 
regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political 
Reform Act. 1/ This letter constitutes written advice under 
Section 84205, which states: 
The Commission may by regulation or written advice 
permit candidates and committees to file campaign 
statements combining statements and reports required 
to be filed by this title. 
A ballot measure election has been scheduled for August 15, 
1989, in connection with two city ordinances. 
Because the first 
pre-election statement due in connection with the election 
(Section 84200.8) and the semi-annual statement due for the first 
six months of 1989 (Section 84200) cover almost identical periods, 
we believe it is appropriate for committees primarily formed to 
support or oppose the measure(s) to combine those two reports. 
Therefore, campaign statements in connection with the August 15, 
1989, election should be filed on the following schedule: 
Type of statement 
Period Covered 
Filing Deadline 
1st Pre-Election (for 
Jan. 1 - July 1 
July 6 
Aug. election) combined 
with Semi-Annual 
2nd Pre-Election for 
July 2 - July 29 
August 3 
Aug. election 
Semi-Annual 
July 30 -
Dec. 31 
January 31, 1990 
l/Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory references 
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission 
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 
18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 2, 
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • 
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • 
(916)322~5660 


Bette Racki 
Page 2 
Candidates and committees not involved in the August 15, 
1989, election will not use this filing schedule, but will use the 
regular dates set out in the "Information Manual on campaign 
Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act ... 
Please call me at (916) 322-5662 if you have any questions. 
Sincerely, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
General Counsel 
By: 
Carla Wardlow 
Assistant Chief, Technical 
Assistance & Analysis Division
